conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by reading the official guidance on Child Benefit eligibility.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you assess your eligibility for Child Benefit. 

To start, I need to know: **How many children are you asking about, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm asking about my son, Alex. He's 20 years old and lives with me here in Bristol.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I'm assessing eligibility for your son, Alex, who is 20 years old and lives with you in Bristol.

Since Alex is 20 years old, Child Benefit for children over 16 has specific rules. According to the guidance, you can normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 **and they stay in approved education or training**.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any sort of education or training at the moment. He finished his studies a while back and has just been figuring out what he wants to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. 

Based on the information you've provided, I now have enough to assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit.

According to the official guidance, Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 (under 20) **only if** they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is 20 years old and is not currently in approved education or training, he does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

The rules state that Child Benefit normally stops when a child turns 16, unless they continue in approved education or training (up until they turn 20). Once a child reaches 20 years old, Child Benefit stops regardless of their circumstances.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**I'm sorry, but you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

The reason is that Alex is 20 years old, which exceeds the maximum age limit for Child Benefit. Child Benefit is available for children under 16, or for young people aged 16-19 (under 20) if they remain in approved education or training. Once a young person turns 20, Child Benefit stops regardless of their circumstances.

If you have any other questions or if there are other children you'd like to ask about, please let me know.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 20 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 20, which is 20 or over
circumstances: Child is 20, which is 20 or over
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 20 years old and not in approved education or training. According to Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16, or for a child aged 16-19 (under 20) if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is 20 years old, he has exceeded the maximum age limit for Child Benefit eligibility. Child Benefit stops when a young person turns 20, regardless of whether they are in education or training.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is asking about their 20-year-old son Alex who lives with them in Bristol but is not in education or training. Child Benefit is not available for children aged 20 or over, so Alex does not qualify.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 20 years old and not in approved education or training. According to Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16, or for a child aged 16-19 (under 20) if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is 20 years old, he has exceeded the maximum age limit for Child Benefit eligibility. Child Benefit stops when a young person turns 20, regardless of whether they are in education or training.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is asking about their 20-year-old son Alex who lives with them in Bristol but is not in education or training. Child Benefit is not available for children aged 20 or over, so Alex does not qualify.
